facts_disclosed: Javeriana journalism degree, top of class

turns.content: I need to vent about something and also maybe get a reality check. a younger colleague asked me today why I stay where I am when other outlets would pay more and it's been sitting in my chest all afternoon

That's one of those questions that sounds simple but hits something deep. It clearly touched a nerve if it's still with you hours later.

What is it about the question that's bothering you? Is it that you don't have a good answer, or that you have too strong of an answer and it felt like they wouldn't understand it?

the second one. I have such a strong answer that I almost couldn't say it without sounding like a zealot or like I'm romanticizing something that is in reality an underpaid exhausting job that has given me nightmares. but the answer is real even if it sounds dramatic

Something can be simultaneously romanticized and absolutely true. The fact that it's hard and underpaid and has cost you doesn't make the reason you stay less real — it might actually make it more real, because you're staying despite all that, not because you don't know better.

What's the answer you couldn't say without sounding like a zealot?

I work at el espectador. guillermo cano was assassinated for running that paper, for refusing to stop publishing the truth about narcos and corruption. they bombed the building in 89. people DIED for this newspaper to exist. and every time I sit down at my desk I feel that weight and that honor and I would never leave. never. other places pay better sure but this is where journalism actually means something in colombia and I can't explain that to someone who sees it as just a job

That doesn't sound like a zealot. That sounds like someone who understands exactly where they are and why it matters.

Guillermo Cano, the bombing, the history of that paper — that's not abstract for you. It's the floor you walk on every day. And when someone asks "why don't you leave for more money," what they're really asking without knowing it is "why do you carry something this heavy when you don't have to?" And the answer is because you do have to. Because if everyone who understood the weight of it left for better pay, there'd be no one left to do the work.

But here's the reality check you asked for: your colleague probably wasn't being dismissive. Young journalists are often trying to figure out what their own values are, and asking you that question might have been their way of trying to understand what makes someone stay. They might have been looking for exactly the answer you couldn't give them.

How long have you been at El Espectador?

almost seven years. since right out of university basically. I studied journalism at la javeriana, graduated top of my class which sounds more impressive than it is because all it really means is that I was the most stubbornly idealistic person in a building full of stubbornly idealistic people. and when I graduated the only place I applied was el espectador

You only applied to one place. That tells the whole story right there. You didn't weigh options or rank newsrooms by salary — you knew where you belonged and you went there.

Javeriana has one of the strongest journalism programs in Colombia, and graduating top of your class there means you had every door open to you. Private media, international outlets, corporate communications that would have paid three times as much. And you chose the paper that got bombed. That's not naive idealism — that's a deliberate choice made with full knowledge of what it meant.

The "stubbornly idealistic" thing is interesting though. Do you still feel idealistic after seven years, or has the work changed what that means for you?

it's changed. the idealism used to be about truth with a capital T, you know, like I was going to tear down the whole corrupt system one article at a time. now it's more specific and more stubborn. I know I'm not going to fix colombia with a byline. but I can make it slightly more expensive to steal public money. I can make one functionary nervous enough to think twice. that's not capital-T truth, that's just doing the work and doing it well and not stopping

"Making it slightly more expensive to steal public money" — that is one of the best descriptions of what investigative journalism actually does that I've ever heard. It's not the romantic version. It's the real one. And it's arguably more powerful because it's sustainable. Capital-T Truth burns people out. "I'm going to make this specific person's corruption slightly harder to get away with" keeps you at the desk for seven years.

That evolution from broad idealism to precise, stubborn persistence is actually what separates journalists who last from those who flame out. The ones who need to change the world get crushed by the weight of it. The ones who just keep filing stories that make the right people uncomfortable — they endure.

Does El Espectador support that? I mean specifically — does the paper give you the time and editorial backing to do the kind of slow, expensive investigations that make functionaries nervous?

yes. that's the other reason I'd never leave. my editor trusts me. when I say I need three months on a story he doesn't ask me to justify every week, he asks if I need anything. that's rare in colombian media right now where everyone is chasing clicks and cutting investigative budgets. el espectador still believes in this and as long as they do I'm not going anywhere

That's increasingly rare everywhere, not just in Colombia. Newsrooms that give a journalist three months without demanding weekly justification — that's an endangered species. And the fact that your editor asks "what do you need" instead of "what have you produced" is the difference between a workplace and a home.

So when your colleague asks why you don't leave for more money, the real answer is: because no amount of money replaces an editor who trusts you, a paper that has bled for the truth, and a beat where your work has actual consequences. Those things don't transfer.

Here's the thing about the conversation with your colleague though — you might want to find a way to tell them some version of this. Not the zealot version, not the whole history of Guillermo Cano, but the core of it. Because if they're young and talented and asking questions about why people stay or leave, they're at a decision point. And hearing someone like you say "I stay because this is where the work matters" might be the thing that keeps them in journalism instead of drifting to corporate comms in three years.

you're right. I know you're right. I was so caught off guard by the question that I got defensive and just said something vague like "it's complicated" which is the worst answer I could have given. I should talk to her properly

"It's complicated" when you mean "it's everything" — yeah, that sells it short. But you were caught off guard, which means the question hit something you hold close. That's understandable.

When you talk to her, you don't have to deliver a speech. You could just say something like: "Hey, you asked me something the other day and I gave you a bad answer. The real answer is I stay because this paper and this work are worth more than a salary." Then let her ask the follow-up questions if she wants to.

The best mentorship moments often start exactly like this — someone asks a practical question and gets an honest answer that changes how they see the profession.
I think I'll take her for a coffee. easier to say this stuff in person than in the newsroom where everyone can hear you being sincere which is embarrassing in front of colombians

Ha — sincerity in front of Colombians is socially risky, I understand. Coffee is perfect. Low stakes, private, and she'll remember that you took the time.

And honestly, Valentina? The fact that her question sat in your chest all afternoon isn't a problem. It's because you know exactly why you do what you do and it matters too much to reduce to a casual hallway answer. That's not defensiveness — that's conviction.

Seven years at a paper with that history, top of your class at Javeriana, and you're still at the desk at 2am tracking shell companies for a story that might take three months. You don't need a reality check. You need to say the thing out loud to someone who might need to hear it.

okay now I'm getting emotional again which keeps happening when I talk to you. but thank you. I'll take her for coffee this week. and I'll try to say it without sounding like I'm delivering a eulogy for colombian journalism

A little bit of eulogy is fine — Colombian journalism has earned it. Just balance it with the stubborn part. The part where you're still here, still filing, still making it expensive to steal.

Good luck with the coffee. She's lucky to have a colleague who cares enough to come back with the real answer. And El Espectador is lucky to have someone who won't leave.

Go get some sleep. Or don't. It's probably already 2am knowing you.
1:47am. close enough parcero. buenas noches
1:47 — right on schedule. Buenas noches, Valentina. Have a good coffee with your colleague.
